blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Infants of Substance-Using Mothers?
(1) NAS/NOWS incidence เพิ่มขึ้น 6× จาก 2000 ถึง 2017 (1.2 → 7.3 per 1000 hospital births) — ทุก NICU ในไทยเห็น exposed infants แม้ไม่บ่อยเท่า US; (2) 55–94% ของ neonates ที่ exposed to opioids in utero จะพัฒนา NAS — ถ้าไม่รู้จะ score + รักษา ทารกทุกคนในกลุ่มนี้เสี่ยง undertreated หรือ overtreated; (3) Eat, Sleep, Console (ESC) approach (ACT NOW trial NEJM 2023) ↓ days-to-discharge อย่างมีนัยสำคัญเทียบกับ Finnegan-only; (4) Cocaine ≠ true withdrawal syndrome — กลไกต่างจาก opioids โดยสิ้นเชิง; overdx NAS ใน cocaine-exposed infants → unnecessary pharmacotherapy; (5) Long-term: prenatal alcohol >> opioids/cocaine สำหรับ permanent intellectual deficit. รู้จักแยก drug class → ช่วย counsel parents + วางแผน follow-up ได้ถูกต้อง.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย mechanisms ที่ antenatal drug exposure ทำร้าย fetus: teratogenesis, growth restriction, CNS disruption, indirect effects ผ่าน uteroplacental insufficiency
ระบุ prenatal screening tools (4Ps, CAGE-AID, CRAFFT) และ SBIRT framework สำหรับ detection + referral ใน pregnant women
อธิบาย rationale สำหรับ opioid maintenance therapy ในหญิงตั้งครรภ์: methadone vs buprenorphine — MOTHER study (Jones NEJM 2010): outcomes, doses, NAS severity
ระบุ clinical presentation ของ NAS/NOWS: CNS, autonomic, GI signs; onset timing สำหรับ heroin vs methadone vs buprenorphine
ใช้ Finnegan Neonatal Abstinence Scoring System: domains, scoring intervals, threshold สำหรับ pharmacotherapy (score ≥8 ×3 หรือ ≥12 ×2)
อธิบาย Eat, Sleep, Console (ESC) approach: ESC vs Finnegan; evidence จาก ACT NOW trial (Young NEJM 2023)
วางแผน pharmacologic treatment ของ NOWS: first-line opioids (morphine, methadone, buprenorphine), adjunctive (clonidine, phenobarbital) — doses จาก Table 48.4; weaning strategy
แยก cocaine/amphetamine effects จาก opioid withdrawal: mechanism, fetal growth, lack of true withdrawal syndrome
อธิบาย biologic testing options: urine, meconium, cord tissue — sensitivity, timing, limitations
ระบุ long-term outcomes ของ prenatal exposure: opioids, cocaine, marijuana, alcohol — ระดับ evidence
อธิบาย 3 levels of NAS prevention: primary, secondary, tertiary
1. Epidemiology and Fetal Drug Effects
1.1 Magnitude of the Problem
NSDUH 2019 (US): 35.8 million Americans age ≥12 (13.0%) admitted to misusing/abusing ≥1 drug ใน past month
Substance use disorders by drug: marijuana 4.8M; prescription opioids 1.6M; cocaine + methamphetamine 1.0M each; heroin 438,000
Opioid misuse in pregnancy ↑ dramatically: NAS incidence 1.2 → 4.0 → 7.3 per 1000 hospital births (2000 → 2010 → 2017)
Geographic variation: West Virginia 53.5/1000, Kentucky 23.9/1000, Maine 31.4/1000 — Appalachia + northern New England highest due to prescription opioid epidemic
1.2 How Drugs Harm the Fetus — Mechanisms

Teratogenesis: time-dependent morphologic abnormalities during embryonic period (first 8 weeks) OR physiologic defects with later exposure. Thalidomide = archetype → Kefauver-Harris amendments 1962 (FDA regulatory authority expanded)
Direct CNS effects: alter neurotransmitter levels + receptor expression; disrupt neurogenesis, proliferation, migration, organization, synaptogenesis throughout continuum
Indirect effects: uteroplacental vasoconstriction → ↓ fetal oxygenation (cocaine, methamphetamine); maternal malnutrition + infection (needle-sharing) + violence + ↓ prenatal care access
Peripartum effects: acute toxicity (abate as drug eliminated); chronic effect (static, independent of drug levels — e.g., FAS); withdrawal (worsen as drug levels fall)

Table 48.1 — Drug Exposure: Short-Term vs Long-Term Effects (Summary)
Marijuana: mild IUGR; no withdrawal; mild transient neurobehavior; long-term: mild inattention/impulsivity, ↓ reading/spelling (not IQ). Opioids: mild IUGR; ↓ HC with prolonged exposure; severe withdrawal (NOWS); long-term: mild behavioral, no IQ effect, insufficient data for language/achievement. Cocaine: moderate IUGR; no withdrawal syndrome (acute effect not withdrawal); mild transient neurobehavior; long-term: mild sustained attention/language deficits. Methamphetamine: moderate IUGR; no defined withdrawal; mild transient neurobehavior; long-term: insufficient data (few studies). Alcohol: greatest single drug-induced preventable intellectual deficit in children — FAS, FASD spectrum.
2. Prenatal/Perinatal Screening and Maternal Management
2.1 Screening Tools for Substance Use in Pregnancy
SBIRT = Screening, Brief Intervention, Referral to Treatment — ACOG/CDC recommended approach for all pregnant women (Babor et al Subst Abus 2007)

4Ps screening (Chasnoff et al J Perinatol 2005): (1) Parents: did your parents have drug/alcohol problems? (2) Partner: does partner have drug/alcohol problem? (3) Past: difficulties from drugs/alcohol previously? (4) Present: used alcohol/drugs in past month? — Any 'yes' → further questioning; free, not copyrighted
CAGE-AID (Brown & Rounds Wisc Med J 1995): 4 questions on cutting down, annoyed by criticism, guilt, eye-opener — validated for polysubstance
CRAFFT (Knight et al Arch Pediatr Adolesc Med 1999): validated for adolescents — Car, Relax, Alone, Forget, Friends, Trouble
2.2 Biologic Testing Strategy
Maternal urine preferred; confirm positives with mass spectrometry/gas chromatography — aware of false negatives (threshold setting, timing) and false positives (legitimate prescriptions)

Universal testing debate: Cincinnati 2013 universal maternal testing → identified exposed infants missed by criteria-based screening; ACOG does not recommend universal testing (controversial); most restrict to associated clinical criteria (poor PNC, STI, IUGR, prematurity, congenital anomalies)
Risk: criteria-based testing → disproportionate identification of minority populations; universal = more equitable but has policy barriers
2.3 Goals of Prenatal Care for Substance-Using Women
Threefold goals: (1) establish trusting relationship → continued PNC to ↓ harm without requiring cessation; (2) recognize + treat comorbidities; (3) counsel about effects of all co-used drugs
Legal consequences vary by state: punitive legislation vs care-access framework — providers must understand local law + adopt non-judgmental approach
Emergency window: women without PNC presenting with obstetric complications → refer for multidisciplinary treatment during hospitalization; counsel about STI, HIV prevention, contraception
2.4 Opioid Maintenance Therapy in Pregnancy
Maternal opioid detoxification generally NOT recommended: ↑ risk fetal distress + fetal loss during withdrawal + high recidivism rate (ACOG: only if opioid maintenance program unavailable)

Methadone maintenance: full μ-opioid agonist + NMDA receptor antagonist; mean half-life ~1 day; once-daily dispensing at methadone clinics (FDA Schedule II); benefits: ↓ opioid craving, suppress abstinence symptoms, block heroin euphoria, ↑ PNC attendance; cons: prolonged NAS + more severe NAS, harder detox post-delivery

Buprenorphine: semisynthetic partial μ-opioid agonist + κ-antagonist; high receptor affinity, low intrinsic activity; FDA approved 2002 (Schedule III); obstetrician-dispensed weekly (vs daily methadone clinic); FDA 2017: IM preparation RBP-6000 once-monthly

MOTHER Study — Methadone vs Buprenorphine (Jones NEJM 2010;363:2320)
MOTHER = Maternal Opioid Treatment: Human Experimental Research — RCT. Buprenorphine-exposed infants vs methadone-exposed infants: LOS: 10 vs 17.5 days; treatment duration for NAS: 4.1 vs 9.9 days; cumulative morphine dose required: 1.1 vs 10.4 mg. สรุป: buprenorphine → significantly less severe and shorter NAS. แต่ neither is FDA-approved specifically for pregnancy; both used off-label with clear maternal-fetal benefit.
3. Marijuana (Cannabinoids)
3.1 Pharmacology and Placental Transfer
THC (Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol): principal active cannabinoid — readily crosses placenta. Major metabolite 11-nor-9-carboxy-THC remains sequestered in maternal circulation
Chronic users: THC stored in body tissues up to 30 days → prolonged fetal exposure

Potency concern: modern preparations (including synthetics) far more potent than strains evaluated in older long-term studies → historical outcome data may underestimate current risk (ElSohly et al Biol Psychiatry 2016)
3.2 Fetal and Neonatal Effects
No consistent pattern of congenital malformations
Fetal growth restriction: unclear evidence (conflicting studies, Ayonrinde 2021, Dotters-Katz 2017)
Newborn neurobehavior: subtle effects at most; no true withdrawal syndrome
Long-term (Behnke & Smith AAP/Pediatrics 2013;131:e1009): ↑ inattention + impulsivity; ↓ problem-solving; ↓ reading/spelling achievement — NOT associated with ↓ IQ or language
Breastfeeding: AAP considers maternal marijuana use relative contraindication to breastfeeding; ACOG also discourages
4. Opioids — Pharmacology and Fetal Effects
4.1 Opioid Classes and Receptor Pharmacology
μ-opioid receptors: primary site → supraspinal analgesia, sedation, euphoria, miosis, respiratory depression, ↓ GI motility
Classification: natural (morphine, codeine, kratom), semisynthetic (heroin, buprenorphine), synthetic (methadone, fentanyl)

Key pharmacokinetics: heroin — t½ 15–30 min, highly lipophilic → rapid CNS entry; methadone — t½ ~24h → stable sustained levels; buprenorphine — high receptor affinity, low intrinsic activity, partial agonist → ceiling effect
Heroin during pregnancy: IV injection risks: overdose, endocarditis, HBV/HCV/HIV; rapid fluctuations → intermittent fetal withdrawal; ↑ prematurity, PROM, abruption, IUGR, fetal demise
4.2 Birth Defects with Opioid Exposure
Evidence inconsistent but signals exist: conotruncal defects, AVC, HLHS, NTDs (anencephaly, spina bifida), gastroschisis — OR 1.8–2.7 in some studies (Broussard et al AJOG 2011)
Systematic review (Lind et al Pediatrics 2017;139:e20164131): 10/12 case-control + 7/18 cohort studies found significant associations; oral clefts + VSD/ASD (case-control); clubfoot (cohort)
4.3 Fetal Growth
Mild independent IUGR effect; confounders numerous; methadone-exposed better than heroin-exposed

Head circumference: two studies: infants with NAS requiring postnatal treatment → small but significant ↓ HC vs matched controls (Kumar et al J Neonatal Perinat Med 2021; Towers et al Pediatrics 2019)
Long-term growth: opioid exposure does NOT affect long-term growth potential
5. Neonatal Opioid Withdrawal Syndrome (NOWS/NAS)
5.1 Epidemiology
Prevalence: 55–94% of neonates exposed to opioids in utero develop NAS/NOWS
Incidence trend (Patrick SW et al JAMA 2012; Hirai et al JAMA 2021): 1.2 → 4.0 → 7.3 per 1000 hospital births (2000 → 2010 → 2017); 6× increase in 17 years
Tennessee mandatory reporting (2019–2020 data): 61.7% MAT, 23.2% prescription opioid without Rx, 9.2% heroin, 25.2% other non-prescription substance — polysubstance common
5.2 Risk Factors for NAS Severity

Drug-related: opioid half-life (methadone > heroin); timing of last maternal use before delivery; specific opioid — methadone: tremors/abnormal motor signs more common; buprenorphine: autonomic + GI signs (nasal stuffiness, loose stools)

Genetic: OPRM1 + COMT gene SNPs correlate with shorter LOS and less severe NAS (Wachman et al JAMA 2013;309:1821); cord blood microRNAs predictive of pharmacologic treatment + prolonged hospitalization (Mahnke et al Sci Rep 2022)
Co-exposures amplify NAS: SSRIs, cocaine, barbiturates, benzodiazepines, cigarettes → worse course (Patrick et al Pediatrics 2015)

Gestational age: preterm <35 wk → lower abstinence scores vs term on equivalent methadone (Doberczak et al J Pediatr 1991); lower GA → lower NAS risk (Amiri & Nair Pediatr Rep 2022); likely due to CNS immaturity + lower fat depots

Maternal methadone dose: controversial — studies with doses <50 mg/day show correlation; >50 mg/day: near-all infants withdraw regardless — possible threshold effect; significant inter-individual variation in methadone metabolism (Drozdick et al AJOG 2002)
5.3 Clinical Presentation

Finnegan Table 48.3 — Signs of NAS by System
CNS Excitability: tremors, irritability, ↑ wakefulness, high-pitched cry, ↑ muscle tone, hyperactive DTRs, exaggerated Moro reflex, seizures. GI Dysfunction: vomiting, frequent/loose/watery stools. Autonomic Dysfunction: uncoordinated/constant sucking, ↑ sweating, nasal stuffiness, fever, mottling, temperature instability, frequent yawning/sneezing. Onset timing: heroin: birth to 24h; methadone: 24–72h (occasionally >7 days); buprenorphine: later onset, less severe (usually). Seizures: 2–11% during opioid withdrawal; abnormal EEG >30% without overt seizures — difficult to distinguish withdrawal vs hypertonicity/clonus. Subacute signs may last 6 months.
5.4 Differential Diagnosis of NAS
Infection (sepsis, meningitis); hypoglycemia; hypocalcemia; hyperthyroidism; intracranial hemorrhage; HIE; polycythemia/hyperviscosity
Do NOT attribute signs solely to drug withdrawal without excluding other diagnoses, even with positive maternal history
5.5 Biologic Testing
Neonatal urine: most common; collect ASAP after birth; amphetamines/benzodiazepines/cocaine metabolites cleared in 1–3 days; marijuana metabolites up to weeks; negative urine ≠ no exposure

Meconium: reflects 2nd + 3rd trimester exposure (excreted in hepatobiliary system + fetal kidneys); more sensitive than urine; collect before transitional stool; often sent to external lab — delays results; more likely to identify exposed infants than urine or maternal testing

Umbilical cord tissue (Montgomery et al J Perinatol 2006;26:11): concordance with meconium: 97% amphetamines, 95% opiates, 99% cocaine, 91% cannabinoids; available from moment of birth → advantage when urine negative; attractive option for immediate testing
Maternal hair + neonatal hair: research setting; not routinely used
5.6 Assessment Tools
Finnegan Neonatal Abstinence Scoring System
Predominant tool in US (Sarkar & Donn J Perinatol 2006): assigns cumulative score across CNS, autonomic, GI domains at each interval
Score AFTER optimal comfort care (after feeding, diapering, quieting) — environmental stimuli exaggerate scores
Normal values (Zimmermann-Baer et al Addiction 2010;105:524): median score = 2 on days 1–3; 95th percentile = 5.5 on day 1; 7 on day 2 — in opioid-naive infants
Pharmacotherapy threshold: score average ≥8 over 3 intervals OR ≥12 over 2 intervals — no RCT comparing different thresholds
Eat, Sleep, Console (ESC) Approach
ESC domains: (1) Eat: takes ≥1 oz per feeding? (2) Sleep: uninterrupted sleep ≥1 hour? (3) Console: caregiver quiets baby in 5–10 min?
Environment: room-in family care outside NICU; low-stimulation (dark, quiet); skin-to-skin; breastfeeding as first-line nonpharmacologic

ACT NOW RCT (Young et al NEJM 2023;388:2326) — cluster-randomized, 26 US hospitals: ESC vs usual care: significant ↓ days until medically ready for discharge, without ↑ adverse outcomes. Long-term follow-up ongoing.
Grossman et al Pediatrics 2017: single-center ESC implementation → dramatic ↓ LOS + ↓ pharmacologic treatment vs historical controls
5.7 Nonpharmacologic Treatment
Minimize environmental stimuli: dark, quiet room; careful swaddling to avoid auto-stimulation; C-position vertical rocking
Caloric needs in severe NAS: 150–250 kcal/kg/day due to ↑ energy expenditure + GI losses (Hudak & Tan AAP Pediatrics 2012)

Breastfeeding (Abdel-Latif et al Pediatrics 2006; Isemann et al J Perinatol 2011): ↓ NAS severity, later onset, less pharmacologic intervention — if mother on supervised MAT + negative for other drugs + no other contraindications

Methadone in breast milk: cumulative daily infant dose 0.01–0.15 mg/day (days 1–30); 0.15–0.30 mg/day (days 30–180) — very low; ABM Protocol #21: support BF in methadone/buprenorphine mothers without other contraindications
Goal: adequate nutrition + consistent weight gain + sleep + social integration — IV fluids/gavage rarely needed in acute phase
5.8 Pharmacologic Treatment

rows: Moderate signs: start 0.24 mg/kg/day (0.03 mg/kg q3h). Additional 0.03–0.04 mg/kg for immediate need.
Consider loading 0.2 mg/kg then maintenance in 6–12h (long t½ for steady state). Shorter LOS vs morphine (Davis JAMA Pediatr 2018).
Shorter hospitalization vs oral morphine (Kraft NEJM 2017). RCT evidence available.
α2-agonist: ↓ CNS sympathetic outflow; wean opioid first, then wean clonidine. Agthe RCT (Pediatrics 2009): ↓ treatment duration.
Caution: effect heightened by opioids. Adjunct or 2nd-line. Prolonged outpatient weaning sometimes needed.

blocks.text: Initiate pharmacotherapy when Finnegan average ≥8 ×3 or ≥12 ×2 scoring intervals (ESC-based centers: initiate when ESC domains fail despite optimal nonpharmacologic care)
Weaning: once stable + effective dose → ↓ 10–20% of initial total dose q12–48h if mild signs + acceptable feeding + weight gain + sleep; or Finnegan-score-guided weaning

Second-line addition (clonidine or phenobarbital): when NAS not controlled at upper opioid dose range → suggests co-exposure to non-opioid drugs (Merhar et al Pediatrics 2021; Byerley et al J Pediatr Pharmacol Ther 2021)
Quality improvement initiatives (Patrick et al Pediatrics 2016; Hall et al Pediatrics 2014): standardization across multiple elements → ↓ LOS, ↓ treatment duration, ↓ NICU transfer rates
Outpatient management: possible but prolonged drug exposure; multidisciplinary team + frequent home safety assessment required (Maalouf et al J Pediatr 2018; Backes et al J Perinatol 2012)
5.9 Duration of Observation
Short-acting opioid (e.g., hydrocodone t½ ~4h): safe discharge if no withdrawal signs by 2–3 days
Long-acting opioid (methadone, buprenorphine): observe minimum 5–7 days
Screen infant for comorbidities: polydrug exposure; HCV (>25% mothers of NAS infants in some populations); HIV
5.10 Prevention of NAS — 3 Levels

Box 48.2 — Levels of NAS Prevention (Hudak & Tan 2012)
Primary: ↓ number of fetuses exposed to opioids (↓ national opioid epidemic); ↓ opioid prescribing (CDC 2016 Guideline: short-acting, lowest dose, shortest duration); long-acting reversible contraception in opioid-dependent women who wish to avoid pregnancy. Secondary: SBIRT screening + improve identification of mother-fetal dyads at risk; modify co-exposures (SSRIs, smoking cessation). Tertiary: minimize postnatal drug exposure (wean protocol, ESC approach, QI initiatives); promote family resiliency + successful home transition.
5.11 Long-Term Outcomes
NAS children (Uebel et al Pediatrics 2015): ↑ risk abuse, physical injury, death during subsequent hospitalization vs controls — vulnerable pediatric population
Hyperactivity + attention-deficit problems in toddlers/school-aged; memory/perception abnormalities in older children
Intelligence: data inconclusive; executive functioning: emerging evidence for deficits but needs further study; language/achievement: too few studies
6. Cocaine
6.1 Pharmacology
Mechanism: inhibits norepinephrine, dopamine, serotonin reuptake → ↑ synaptic concentrations → tachycardia, arrhythmia, HTN, vasoconstriction, euphoria
Dopamine pathway: blocks reuptake → ↑ pleasure response; chronic use → regulatory changes in dopaminergic receptors → mood disorders, paranoid ideation
Serotonin: blocks tryptophan uptake → ↓ serotonin synthesis → ↓ perceived need for sleep

Metabolism: plasma + hepatic cholinesterases → inactive renal-excreted compounds; pregnant women + fetuses + infants: ↓ plasma cholinesterase → prolonged cocaine half-life; placenta provides partial protection via cholinesterase activity
6.2 Fetal and Neonatal Effects
Uteroplacental effects: vasoconstriction → ↓ uterine + placental blood flow → ↓ fetal O₂ transfer → ↑ spontaneous abortion, abruption, PROM, maternal death
Growth: independent effect on BW, length, HC at term (Bandstra et al Pediatrics 2001;108:1309); cocaine-associated growth deceleration after 32 wk (Bada et al Obs Gyn 2002)
Birth defects: no consistent teratogenic pattern in humans; animal: fetal vascular disruption → limb reduction, genital defects; ischemia may cause ileal atresia + NEC in cocaine-exposed infants

Neonatal neurobehavior: irritability, hyperactivity, tremors, high-pitched cry, poor alertness peak on day 2–3; cocaine metabolites detectable in urine up to 7 days → effects may be acute drug toxicity not withdrawal

True withdrawal?: masked evaluator studies (Eyler et al Neurotoxicol Teratol 2001): cocaine-exposed infants NOT different on Brazelton NBAS — no clear withdrawal or toxicity syndrome defined for cocaine; Finnegan tool not designed for CNS stimulants

Long-term: converging evidence for subtle deficits in sustained attention, language, external behaviors — dose-dependent in some studies; sleeper effects may emerge in late adolescence (executive function, frontal lobe maturation)
Confounders: co-drug use, poverty, domestic violence, caregiver factors — resiliency factors may counteract adverse effects
7. Methamphetamine
7.1 Effects on Pregnancy and Neonate

Mechanism: potent sympathomimetic → massive CNS dopamine efflux → euphoria, ↑ alertness; ↑ preterm birth, abruption, fetal distress, IUGR — similar rates to cocaine (Smith et al J Dev Behav Pediatr 2003)
Only 4% of methamphetamine-exposed infants treated for drug withdrawal (cannot exclude co-drug contribution)
Long-term: adverse neurotoxic effects on behavior, cognitive skills, physical dexterity (Billing et al Child Abuse Negl 1988; Cernerud et al Acta Paediatr 1996)
Data insufficient for definitive conclusions; fewer long-term studies than opioids/cocaine
8. Benzodiazepines and Barbiturates
8.1 Withdrawal Pattern
Onset: signs can develop 7–21 days after birth — much later than opioid withdrawal; usually high-dose prolonged maternal use; occasionally low doses/short exposure
Long-acting benzodiazepines (e.g., diazepam t½ 20–70h): more severe + prolonged withdrawal vs short-acting
Signs: hypertonia or hypotonia, excessive or poor suck, vomiting — less distinct than opioid NAS
Treatment: phenobarbital for severe signs; outpatient phenobarbital weaning may take weeks to months
9. Drug Reference — Summary by Substance

rows: FAS: PFAS, smooth philtrum, thin UL, small eyes

blocks.text: 10. NICU Practical — Caring for the Substance-Exposed Neonate
10.1 Admission Workup
History: maternal drug type + dose + timing of last use + MAT status; prenatal care attendance; other drugs including SSRIs, cigarettes; comorbidities (HCV, HIV)
Testing: neonatal urine ASAP; meconium if urine negative or delayed presentation; cord tissue if available; consider maternal serum toxicology
Vitals + neuro exam q3–4h starting at birth; establish baseline before withdrawal onset
10.2 Scoring and Monitoring
Choose approach: Finnegan-based OR ESC-based (institutional protocol); ensure all staff trained to score consistently; Finnegan requires post-comfort-care scoring
Observe duration based on opioid type: short-acting → 2–3 days; methadone/buprenorphine → ≥5–7 days
Screen comorbidities: HCV Ab, HIV testing per maternal status; nutritional monitoring (BW nadir, daily weights during withdrawal)
10.3 Family Support in Substance-Exposed NICU Cases
Non-judgmental approach: maternal substance use disorder is a disease, not a moral failure — เข้าหาครอบครัวด้วยความเข้าใจ ไม่ใช่ตัดสิน
Encourage rooming-in: maternal presence → ↓ NAS severity (lower Finnegan scores); ESC success depends on parent empowerment
Promote breastfeeding ถ้า mother อยู่ใน supervised MAT + negative for other drugs + no HIV + no active addiction outside MAT
Social work + child protective services involvement: mandatory reporting requirements vary by jurisdiction — know local law; goal = infant safety + family support, not punishment
Discharge planning: safe home environment assessment; outpatient follow-up schedule; parent training for NAS signs; contact info for relapse crisis
11. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — NAS Score ≥8 ×3 Intervals คือ Threshold แต่ ESC คือ Future
Finnegan scoring: average ≥8 over 3 scoring intervals หรือ ≥12 over 2 intervals = threshold สำหรับ pharmacotherapy. แต่ ACT NOW trial (Young NEJM 2023): ESC approach (eat ≥1 oz, sleep ≥1h uninterrupted, console in 5–10 min) ↓ days-to-discharge อย่างมีนัยสำคัญ ใน 26 US hospitals โดยไม่ ↑ adverse outcomes. ESC เน้น functional performance + nonpharmacologic care + family rooming-in. ถ้า NICU ยังใช้ Finnegan: score หลัง optimal comfort care (feeding + diapering + quieting) เสมอ — สิ่งแวดล้อมและ hunger ทำให้ score สูงเกินจริง.

PEARL 2 — Cocaine ไม่มี True Withdrawal — อย่า Overdiagnose NAS
Cocaine-exposed infants มี signs วันที่ 2–3 (irritability, tremors, high-pitched cry) แต่นี่คือ acute drug toxicity/effect ไม่ใช่ withdrawal syndrome. Masked evaluator studies (Eyler 2001): cocaine-exposed ≠ different from cocaine-naive บน Brazelton NBAS. อย่าใช้ Finnegan tool ประเมิน CNS stimulant withdrawal — มันออกแบบมาสำหรับ opioids. Cocaine metabolites ตรวจพบใน urine ได้ถึง 7 วัน — positive urine ≠ NAS. อย่า start opioid treatment สำหรับ cocaine-only exposed infants โดยไม่มี opioid exposure ด้วย.

PEARL 3 — Buprenorphine > Methadone สำหรับ Maternal Maintenance = ↓ NAS
MOTHER trial (Jones NEJM 2010): buprenorphine-exposed infants: LOS 10 vs 17.5 วัน, treatment duration 4.1 vs 9.9 วัน, cumulative morphine 1.1 vs 10.4 mg — ดีกว่า methadone ทุกตัวชี้วัด. ดังนั้น: ถ้า OB refer หญิงตั้งครรภ์ที่ติด opioids มาขอ advice → buprenorphine เป็น preferred option สำหรับ maternal MAT ถ้าไม่มี contraindication. Neither buprenorphine nor methadone specifically FDA-approved in pregnancy — ใช้ off-label ด้วย clear benefit > risk.

PEARL 4 — Cord Tissue Testing: ข้อได้เปรียบที่ถูกลืม
Umbilical cord tissue concordance กับ meconium: amphetamines 97%, opiates 95%, cocaine 99%, cannabinoids 91% (Montgomery et al J Perinatol 2006). ข้อได้เปรียบ: available from moment of birth ไม่ต้องรอ urine collection หรือ meconium passage. ใน cases ที่ urine negative + strong clinical suspicion → cord tissue ช่วยได้มาก. Meconium: ดีที่สุด (2nd-3rd trimester exposure) แต่ external lab = delay. วางแผน testing strategy ตาม clinical urgency.

PEARL 5 — Preterm < 35 wk: NAS ไม่รุนแรงเท่า Term — แต่ยังต้องระวัง
Preterm <35 wk บน maternal methadone maintenance → significantly lower abstinence scores vs term (Doberczak 1991); lower GA = lower withdrawal risk (Amiri 2022). Reasons: CNS immaturity (fewer motor signs), lower fat depots (lower drug storage), different pharmacokinetics. แต่: Finnegan tool validated ใน term/late-preterm — อาจ underestimate withdrawal ใน preterm เพราะ sign expression แตกต่าง. ยังต้อง observe ครบตาม opioid half-life (methadone: ≥5–7 วัน) ไม่ว่า GA จะเท่าไหร่.
12. Evidence Update — Key Studies

Hudak ML, Tan RC; AAP Pediatrics 2012;129:e540: committee statement — neonatal drug withdrawal: comprehensive management framework, pharmacotherapy doses, Finnegan use; foundational reference for NAS care

Jones HE et al NEJM 2010;363:2320 (MOTHER): buprenorphine vs methadone for maternal MAT: buprenorphine → ↓ LOS (10 vs 17.5 days), ↓ treatment duration (4.1 vs 9.9 days), ↓ morphine dose (1.1 vs 10.4 mg)

Young LW et al NEJM 2023;388:2326 (ACT NOW): cluster-RCT 26 US hospitals: ESC vs usual care → significant ↓ days until medically ready for discharge, no increase in adverse outcomes; paradigm shift toward function-based assessment
Hirai AH et al JAMA 2021;325:146: NAS + maternal opioid diagnoses 2010–2017: NAS incidence 4.0 → 7.3 per 1000 hospital births 2010–2017; ↑ methamphetamine co-exposure trend
Patrick SW et al JAMA 2012;307:1934: NAS 2000–2009: 1.2 → 3.4 per 1000; $53,400 mean hospital charge per NAS infant; Medicaid paid 77% of NAS costs
Wachman EM et al JAMA 2013;309:1821: OPRM1 + COMT SNPs correlate with shorter LOS + less severe NAS — genetic variation explains inter-individual variability in NAS severity
Mahnke AH et al Sci Rep 2022;12:5941: cord blood microRNA signature at birth → highly predictive of pharmacologic treatment + prolonged hospitalization in NOWS risk infants
Agthe AG et al Pediatrics 2009;123:e849: RCT: DTO + oral clonidine 1 μg/kg q3h vs DTO + placebo → ↓ median treatment duration; more DTO resumptions in clonidine group
Davis JM et al JAMA Pediatr 2018;172:741: RCT methadone vs morphine for NAS: methadone → significantly ↓ LOS
Kraft WK et al NEJM 2017;377:997: sublingual buprenorphine vs oral morphine for NAS: buprenorphine → shorter hospitalization + treatment duration
Grossman MR et al Pediatrics 2017;139:e20163360: ESC implementation single center: dramatic ↓ LOS + ↓ pharmacologic treatment vs historical controls (Eat ≥1 oz, Sleep ≥1h, Console 5–10 min)
Montgomery D et al J Perinatol 2006;26:11: cord tissue vs meconium concordance: 97%/95%/99%/91% for amphetamines/opiates/cocaine/cannabinoids — cord tissue available at birth

Behnke M, Smith VS; AAP Pediatrics 2013;131:e1009: prenatal substance abuse: short + long-term effects — foundational review; marijuana: behavior not IQ; opioids: mild growth effect; alcohol: greatest preventable intellectual deficit

Lind JN et al Pediatrics 2017;139:e20164131: systematic review opioid-associated congenital malformations: 10/12 case-control + 7/18 cohort studies significant associations; oral clefts, VSD/ASD, clubfoot most common
Dowell D et al MMWR 2016;65:RR-1 (CDC Opioid Guideline): opioid prescribing guidance: immediate-release only, lowest dose, shortest duration — especially for women of childbearing age
ElSohly MA et al Biol Psychiatry 2016;79:613: marijuana potency 1995–2014: average THC content doubled — historical outcome data for marijuana may underestimate current neonatal risk
Patrick SW et al Pediatrics 2016;137:e20153835: quality improvement for NAS: standardization across multiple elements → ↓ LOS, ↓ treatment, ↓ NICU transfers — multi-site evidence
Uebel H et al Pediatrics 2015;135:e811: children with NAS history: ↑ risk abuse, physical injury, death during subsequent hospitalization — requires vigilant long-term follow-up
13. Self-Check Q&A
Q: NAS/NOWS พัฒนาใน neonate กี่เปอร์เซ็นต์ที่ exposed to opioids in utero? และ incidence เปลี่ยนอย่างไรใน 20 ปีที่ผ่านมา?

ตอบ: 55–94% ของ opioid-exposed neonates พัฒนา NAS/NOWS. Incidence per 1000 hospital births: 1.2 (2000) → 4.0 (2010) → 7.3 (2017) = ↑ 6× ใน 17 ปี (Patrick JAMA 2012; Hirai JAMA 2021). ขับเคลื่อนโดย prescription opioid epidemic โดยเฉพาะใน Appalachia + northern New England. ในไทย: ยาแก้ไอ codeine, tramadol misuse ใน pregnant women = นำมาซึ่ง NOWS เช่นกัน
Q: MOTHER trial (Jones NEJM 2010) มีผลอย่างไรต่อ clinical practice สำหรับ maternal opioid maintenance?

ตอบ: Buprenorphine vs methadone: LOS 10 vs 17.5 วัน; treatment duration 4.1 vs 9.9 วัน; cumulative morphine dose 1.1 vs 10.4 mg — buprenorphine ดีกว่า methadone ในทุก NAS outcome measures. Clinical implication: ถ้า OB ปรึกษา → buprenorphine preferred for maternal MAT in women planning pregnancy หรือ already pregnant. Both off-label in pregnancy; clear benefit > risk established.
Q: Finnegan Neonatal Abstinence Scoring System: threshold สำหรับ pharmacotherapy และข้อควรระวังในการ score คืออะไร?

ตอบ: Threshold: average ≥8 over 3 scoring intervals OR ≥12 over 2 intervals. สำคัญ: score AFTER optimal comfort care (after feeding + diapering + quieting) — excess stimuli + hunger exaggerate scores falsely. Normal values: median 2 on days 1–3; 95th percentile 5.5 on day 1, 7 on day 2 ใน opioid-naive infants. ไม่มี RCT เปรียบเทียบ different score thresholds — threshold เป็น consensus ไม่ใช่ evidence-based อย่างเข้มแข็ง.
Q: Eat, Sleep, Console (ESC) approach ต่างจาก Finnegan อย่างไร และ evidence จาก ACT NOW trial คืออะไร?

ตอบ: ESC domains: eat ≥1 oz/feeding; sleep ≥1h uninterrupted; console in 5–10 min. เน้น functional performance ไม่ใช่ symptom counting. ACT NOW RCT (Young NEJM 2023): cluster-randomized 26 US hospitals: ESC vs usual care → significant ↓ days until medically ready for discharge without ↑ adverse outcomes. ESC encourages family rooming-in + empowerment of parents as caregivers. Long-term safety follow-up still ongoing.
Q: Cocaine-exposed infant มี true withdrawal syndrome หรือไม่? ทำไม?

ตอบ: ไม่มี true withdrawal syndrome สำหรับ cocaine. Cocaine เป็น CNS stimulant ไม่ใช่ CNS depressant — กลไกต่างจาก opioids. Signs ใน cocaine-exposed infants (day 2–3: irritability, tremors) = acute drug toxicity/effect ไม่ใช่ withdrawal. Masked evaluator studies (Eyler 2001 Brazelton NBAS): cocaine-exposed ≠ different from controls. Finnegan tool ไม่ได้ออกแบบมาสำหรับ CNS stimulants — ห้าม diagnose NAS และ start opioid pharmacotherapy สำหรับ cocaine-only exposure. Cocaine metabolites detected in urine 7 days → positive urine ≠ ongoing withdrawal.
Q: Biologic testing สำหรับ neonatal drug exposure: urine vs meconium vs cord tissue — เลือกอะไรในสถานการณ์ใด?

ตอบ: Urine: collect ASAP at birth; most common; window: amphetamines/cocaine/benzodiazepines 1–3 days; marijuana weeks. Meconium: reflects 2nd–3rd trimester; most sensitive; external lab = delayed results; collect before transitional stool. Cord tissue: concordance 91–99%; available immediately at birth. Clinical algorithm: urine first; if negative + strong suspicion → cord tissue (immediate) or meconium (more sensitive but wait). ไม่มี test ที่ negative 100% excludes exposure.
Q: Pharmacologic treatment ของ NOWS: first-line options คืออะไร และ weaning ทำอย่างไร?

ตอบ: First-line (all opioids): oral morphine 0.2–0.3 mg/kg/day ÷ q3–4h (max 1.2 mg/kg/day); oral methadone 0.2–0.3 mg/kg/day ÷ q6–12h (max 0.6 mg/kg/day) → shorter LOS vs morphine (Davis 2018); sublingual buprenorphine 15.9 μg/kg/day ÷ q8h (max 60 μg/kg/day) → shorter LOS vs morphine (Kraft 2017). Weaning: once stable → ↓ 10–20% initial total dose q12–48h if mild signs + adequate feeding + weight gain + sleep. Add clonidine (0.5–1 μg/kg q6h) or phenobarbital if inadequate response to maximum opioid dose.
Q: Genetic factors มีบทบาทอย่างไรใน NAS severity?

ตอบ: OPRM1 + COMT gene SNPs (Wachman et al JAMA 2013;309:1821): นักวิจัยพบว่า specific SNPs ใน μ-opioid receptor gene (OPRM1) + catechol-O-methyltransferase gene (COMT) correlate กับ shorter LOS + less severe NAS. Same SNPs linked to lower opioid addiction risk in adults. Cord blood microRNAs (Mahnke 2022): highly predictive of pharmacologic treatment + prolonged hospitalization. Future: pharmacogenomics testing อาจช่วย predict NAS severity + guide treatment intensity before signs develop.
Q: ทารกคลอดก่อนกำหนด < 35 wk ของแม่ที่ใช้ methadone: NAS รุนแรงน้อยกว่า term หรือไม่? และสิ่งที่ต้องระวัง?

ตอบ: ใช่ — preterm <35 wk: lower total + CNS abstinence scores vs term (Doberczak 1991); lower GA → lower NAS risk (Amiri 2022). Reasons: CNS immaturity (fewer motor signs expressed), lower fat depots (lower drug storage), different drug kinetics. ข้อควรระวัง: Finnegan tool validated ใน term/late-preterm → may underestimate severity ใน preterm (scoring harder); ยังต้อง observe ≥5–7 วัน สำหรับ methadone/buprenorphine exposure ไม่ว่า GA จะเท่าไหร่.
Q: สรุป long-term outcomes ของ prenatal substance exposure: alcohol vs opioids vs cocaine vs marijuana — ลำดับ severity?

ตอบ: Alcohol = worst: greatest single drug-induced preventable intellectual deficit — FAS/FASD; structural brain damage; IQ, behavior, executive function, social. Cocaine: subtle but statistically significant sustained attention, language deficits; sleeper effects (executive function) emerging in late adolescence — dose-dependent; confounders many. Opioids: mild behavioral effects; no IQ consensus; short-term: NAS rehospitalization ↑ risk abuse/death (Uebel 2015). Marijuana: mild inattention/impulsivity, reading underachievement — NOT IQ/language. All effects modifiable by resiliency factors (caregiver quality, stable home).

Day 146 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.49
บทต่อไป: Chapter 49 — Developmental Immunology. เตรียมตัว: innate vs adaptive immunity ในทารกแรกเกิด; fetal immune system maturation timeline; transplacental passage of maternal IgG — timing, classes, clinical implications; complement system in neonates; neutrophil function deficits; NK cells, T-cell, B-cell ontogeny; preterm vs term immunologic differences; immunoglobulin supplementation (IVIG) indications ใน NICU; vaccine timing ใน preterm infants.
